retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Making a correct and early diagnosis of Rotor syndrome is crucial, as misdiagnoses of this disease often lead to a more costly, lengthy, and invasive workup that can place a patient at risk of avoidable complications and financial burden. As this disease is generally diagnosed in the pediatric population, it is important to reassure concerned family members that Rotor syndrome is benign with no effect on life expectancy and the occasional appearance of jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome is a benign condition and persists for life. There are no recommended management options for patients suffering from Rotor syndrome. There is no morbidity or mortality associated with Rotor syndrome; hence its prognosis is good. Mortality and morbidity occur if there is another coexisting liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Based on some distinguishing features, Rotor syndrome can be differentiated from other similar disorders, such as Dubin–Johnson syndrome. For instance, the liver has black pigmentation in Dubin–Johnson syndrome, while in Rotor syndrome liver has normal histology and appearance.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome is largely a diagnosis of exclusion. Serological abnormalities in Rotor syndrome only include elevated total serum bilirubin (typically elevated between 2 to 5 mg/dL but may be as high as 20 mg/dL). Most of the time, alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, gamma-glutamyl transferase, and alkaline phosphatase levels are normal, but mild elevations can be seen. If any of these lab values are markedly elevated, investigation for other, more serious conditions is warranted. Imaging studies cannot diagnose Rotor syndrome but can help rule out other diseases that cause hyperbilirubinemia. For example, an ultrasound of the liver and biliary tree can help investigate the causes of extra-hepatic biliary obstruction. The gallbladder is visualized on oral cholecystography in Rotor syndrome, while it is not visualized in DJS. [19] Ultimately, the best method of diagnosing the disease is the analysis of urine coproporphyrin excretion. The total urine coproporphyrin excretion in Rotor syndrome has a two to five-fold elevation, with 65% constituting coproporphyrin 1. [2] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome is a benign disease requiring no treatment. Jaundice is a lifelong finding, but the disease is not associated with morbidity or mortality, and life expectancy is not affected. Most individuals with Rotor syndrome are born to consanguineous couples, and its diagnosis may coincidently identify consanguinity. Distinguishing Rotor syndrome from other more serious disorders is important to avoid unnecessary workup and interventions. It is also critical to reassure and calm patients or family members of patients with Rotor syndrome that the condition is benign. [2] [9] [13] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact etiology of Rotor syndrome (RS) is unknown but appears to be attributable to a deficiency in the intra-cellular capacity of the liver to store organic anions, such as bilirubin diglucuronide. [4] Rotor syndrome is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by homozygous mutations in both the SLCO1B1 and SLCO1B3 genes on chromosome 12. These genes provide instructions for making organic anion-transporting polypeptides 1B1 and 1B3 (OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, respectively). These proteins are found in liver cells and mediate sodium-independent cellular uptake of compounds, including bilirubin glucuronide, bile acids, steroid and thyroid hormones, numerous drugs, toxins, and their conjugates. They share several structural features, such as similar 12 transmembrane domains with intracellularly situated N- and C-termini. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A liver biopsy is not required to diagnose Rotor syndrome, but if done, a liver biopsy in patients with the disease reveals normal histology. In addition, a liver biopsy may help distinguish Rotor syndrome from other, more severe liver diseases. Since Rotor syndrome is clinically similar to Dubin-Johnson syndrome (DJS), it is imperative to differentiate between these two conditions; the absence of dark melanin-like pigments on liver biopsy distinguishes Rotor syndrome from DJS. The outcomes for patients with Rotor syndrome are excellent.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to differentiate Rotor syndrome from other diseases causing hyperbilirubinemia. Normal alkaline phosphatase levels and gamma-glutamyltranspeptidase help distinguish Rotor syndrome from disorders associated with biliary obstruction. Abnormal urinary coproporphyrin excretion and normal liver histology help distinguish this entity from DJS. [18] [17]

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: There are no specific recommendations for patients suffering from Rotor syndrome; however, patients should be given reassurance that the disease is benign and that the jaundice is not associated with progressive liver damage.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The defective storage and excretion of organic anions, such as bilirubin, lead to impaired secretion of conjugated bilirubin, which leads to its reabsorption into the bloodstream and subsequent excretion in the urine. The resulting hyperbilirubinemia manifests as jaundice. As opposed to Dubin-Johnson syndrome, hepatic pigmentation has not been reported.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: In a normal liver, a majority of bilirubin is conjugated by hepatocytes and secreted back into the blood. It is then reabsorbed in downstream hepatocytes by the OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 proteins. [6] In Rotor syndrome, the OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 proteins are abnormally short; therefore, the bilirubin is less efficiently taken up by the liver and removed from the body, causing a buildup of bilirubin in the blood and urine, which results in jaundice and dark urine. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of Rotor syndrome. Outline the presentation of a patient with Rotor syndrome. Summarize the treatment for Rotor syndrome. Explain the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by Rotor syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with Rotor syndrome are typically asymptomatic; however, generalized non-pruritic jaundice can present at birth or early childhood. These symptoms may come and go. Some patients only have scleral icterus. Patients also complain about passing dark-colored urine, long-term jaundice, and fatigue. Additionally, around 5% to 30% of patients may also experience abdominal pain, gastric mucosal abnormalities, and fever. [2] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome, also known as Rotor type hyperbilirubinemia, is an autosomal recessive disease and a rare cause of mixed direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia. The disease is characterized by non-hemolytic jaundice due to chronic elevation of predominantly conjugated bilirubin. This phenomenon results from impaired hepatocellular storage of conjugated bilirubin that leaks into plasma, causing hyperbilirubinemia. Its presenting symptom is jaundice, but Rotor syndrome is a benign, self-limiting disorder that does not require treatment. This activity describes the pathophysiology and presentation of rotor syndrome and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Rotor Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Rotor syndrome is a benign disease with no effect on life expectancy. No adverse drug effects have been documented in people with Rotor syndrome, but the absence of the hepatic proteins OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 may lead to serious issues with liver uptake. OATP1B1 plays a role in drug detoxification, and with reduced activity of this protein, certain drugs such as anticancer agents, methotrexate, and statins can accumulate and result in drug toxicity. [21] Caution should be taken before administering these drugs. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: induction, arrhythmogenic focus ablation, and treatment of ventricular tachycardia target the intrinsic accelerated ventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل) and its subtype ventricular acceleration (التسارع البطيني). Understanding these interconnected entities—electrical conduction abnormalities, structural cardiac changes, specific arrhythmic syndromes, diagnostic measurements, and therapeutic interventions—is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of patients with cardiac rhythm disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2870
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are classified under the broader category تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ and frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية. Recognizing that cardiac dysrhythmias belong to the supraventricular tachycardia family and are often associated with atrial flutter helps clinicians anticipate related rhythm disturbances, guide diagnostic evaluation, and tailor therapeutic strategies for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2747
retrieved_docs.content: Congestive cardiomyopathy (اعتلال عضلة القلب الاحتقاني) is clinically associated with both supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) and atrial flutter (رفرفة أذينية). These arrhythmic conditions frequently arise in the setting of congestive cardiomyopathy, reflecting a close pathophysiological link in which impaired myocardial function predisposes patients to rapid supraventricular rhythms and organized atrial flutter circuits. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as the presence of supraventricular tachycardia or atrial flutter may signal underlying congestive cardiomyopathy and guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at both the structural heart disease and its rhythm complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical association between اضطراب النظام (systemic disorder) and تدلي الصمام المترالي (mitral valve prolapse) indicates that patients who exhibit systemic dysregulation may have an increased likelihood of developing mitral valve prolapse, a condition in which the mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole. Recognizing this link helps clinicians consider valvular assessment in individuals with systemic disorders, facilitating earlier diagnosis and more comprehensive management of potential cardiac involvement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2946
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy), indicating that structural changes in the left ventricle often accompany abnormal heart rhythms. In addition, cardiac dysrhythmias frequently co‑occur with رفرفة أذينية (atrial flutter), reflecting a common overlap of rhythm disturbances that can influence diagnosis, management, and prognosis in patients with cardiac electrical abnormalities. Recognizing the link between cardiac dysrhythmias, left ventricular hypertrophy, and atrial flutter is important for comprehensive cardiac assessment and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This condition involves abnormal dilation of blood vessels that is present from birth and is considered within the spectrum of diseases affecting heart valves. Recognizing congenital vascular dilatation as a valvular heart disease aids clinicians in diagnosing and monitoring potential valve dysfunction, altered hemodynamics, and related complications such as heart failure or arrhythmias, thereby guiding appropriate management and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2765
retrieved_docs.content: CARDIAC CONDUCTION DISEASE WITH OR WITHOUT DILATED CARDIOMYOPATHY is known to have cardiac dysrhythmias as a clinical manifestation. Cardiac dysrhythmias represent a broad spectrum of abnormal heart rhythm disorders, and within this spectrum the specific arrhythmia supraventricular tachycardia (تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ) is classified. Understanding that supraventricular tachycardia is a type of cardiac dysrhythmia clarifies the hierarchical relationship among these concepts and aids clinicians in diagnosing and managing patients who present with conduction disease, possible dilated cardiomyopathy, and associated arrhythmic events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Heart rate and rhythm disorders are classified as a subset of conduction disorders, indicating that these arrhythmic conditions arise from abnormalities in the cardiac electrical conduction system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) is clinically associated with valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that patients with valvular lesions may present with this acute coronary syndrome. Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) occurs within the spectrum of valvular heart disease, reflecting abnormal displacement of the mitral leaflets that contributes to valve dysfunction. Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease, highlighting that certain vascular anomalies are considered part of the broader valvular disease category. Understanding how unstable angina, mitral valve prolapse, and congenital vascular dilatation relate to valvular heart disease helps clinicians assess and manage patients with complex cardiac pathology more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1148
retrieved_docs.content: valve pathologies, broader cardiac disease categories, diagnostic modalities and therapeutic interventions is essential for accurate diagnosis, optimal treatment planning and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 944
retrieved_docs.content: be used to treat تسرع القلب الانتيابي, but it is contraindicated in إِحْصار القلب غير محدد, indicating that while adenosine can terminate certain tachycardias, it should be avoided in patients with unspecified heart block due to the risk of exacerbating conduction failure. Together, these relationships delineate a network in which various rhythm disorders, their anatomical substrates, therapeutic options, and contraindications are interlinked, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing cardiac conduction and rhythm abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2833
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac dysrhythmias, which are classified as a subcategory of تَسَارُّعُ فَوقَ البُطَينِيّ (supraventricular tachycardia), are clinically associated with تضخم البطين الأيسر (left ventricular hypertrophy). This connection suggests that individuals experiencing cardiac dysrhythmias often show structural changes in the left ventricle, underscoring the need to assess ventricular hypertrophy when diagnosing and treating supraventricular tachyarrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: مُعَجَّل) includes ventricular acceleration disorders (التسارع البطيني), and the electrophysiologic procedures of arrhythmia induction, focus ablation, and ventricular tachycardia treatment are specifically directed at managing ventricular tachycardia (التسارع البطيني). Together, these interconnected entities and procedures support the assessment, classification, and therapeutic intervention of cardiac electrical disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 183
retrieved_docs.content: acceleration (التسارع البطيني), which itself is a subtype of an accelerated idioventricular rhythm (نظْمٌ بُطَينِيٌّ ذاتِيٌّ مُعَجَّل). Together, these concepts illustrate how electrocardiographic monitoring and cardiac electrophysiology studies are interrelated tools for diagnosing conduction abnormalities, assessing arrhythmic risk, guiding therapeutic ablation, and supporting cardiac rehabilitation, thereby informing comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884
retrieved_docs.content: Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that this condition involves abnormal movement of the mitral valve leaflets and falls within the broader spectrum of diseases affecting cardiac valves. Recognizing mitral valve prolapse as a type of valvular heart disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and appropriate management of patients with cardiac valve abnormalities.
